questions.question: Why do democratic governments need institutions?
questions.answer: Democratic governments need institutions to ensure decisions follow rules and involve many people.

questions.question: What role did Parliament play in the Mandal Commission decision?
questions.answer: Parliament discussed the report and created pressure on the government to implement it.

questions.question: Why are institutions sometimes seen as causing delays?
questions.answer: Because decision-making involves procedures, discussions and consultations.

questions.question: Why is the Lok Sabha more powerful than the Rajya Sabha?
questions.answer: Because it controls the Council of Ministers and has greater power in financial matters.

questions.question: What happens when the Lok Sabha passes a vote of no confidence?
questions.answer: The Prime Minister and Council of Ministers must resign.

questions.question: Why is the Prime Minister considered the most powerful institution?
questions.answer: Because he leads the Cabinet and coordinates the work of the government.

questions.question: Why do ministers have the final authority in policy decisions?
questions.answer: Because they are elected representatives responsible to the people.

questions.question: Why do civil servants remain in office even when governments change?
questions.answer: Because they are permanent executives appointed for administration.

questions.question: Why does the President act on the advice of the Council of Ministers?
questions.answer: Because the Constitution requires the President to follow the advice of the elected government.

questions.question: Why is the judiciary independent in a democracy?
questions.answer: To ensure fair judgments without influence from the government.

questions.question: Why is the Supreme Court called the guardian of the Constitution?
questions.answer: Because it protects the Constitution and fundamental rights.

questions.question: What is meant by checks and balances in government?
questions.answer: It means different institutions limit each other's powers.

questions.question: Why are parliamentary debates important?
questions.answer: They allow discussion of public issues and government policies.

questions.question: Why can the judiciary strike down laws?
questions.answer: Because courts can review laws under judicial review.

questions.question: Why are Cabinet meetings important?
questions.answer: They help ministers collectively take major policy decisions.

questions.question: Why does Parliament control government finances?
questions.answer: To ensure public money is spent responsibly.

questions.question: Why do courts settle disputes between citizens and government?
questions.answer: To ensure justice and protect citizens’ rights.

questions.question: Why is Rajya Sabha important in a federal system?
questions.answer: Because it represents the interests of states.

questions.question: Why do democratic governments avoid concentrating power in one person?
questions.answer: To prevent misuse of power.

questions.question: Why are ministers accountable to Parliament?
questions.answer: Because they must maintain the confidence of the Lok Sabha.

questions.question: Why is the Cabinet called the inner ring of the Council of Ministers?
questions.answer: Because it consists of senior ministers who take key decisions.

questions.question: Why can courts hear disputes between states?
questions.answer: Because the Supreme Court has authority over such disputes.

questions.question: Why are rules and procedures important in institutions?
questions.answer: They ensure fair and transparent decision-making.

questions.question: Why do institutions provide stability in governance?
questions.answer: Because they ensure continuity even when leaders change.

questions.question: Why do citizens approach courts for Public Interest Litigation?
questions.answer: To protect public interest and address issues affecting society.
